title: The Shadow at the Door
content:
The mountain swallowed the last light of day.

Chen Yuan stood at the temple gate, his robes damp with sweat from the climb, and wondered if he'd made a terrible mistake. The wooden structure before him sagged like an old man's spine, its red paint flaking away in sheets. Weeds choked the stone path leading to the main hall. A broken prayer flag flapped in the evening wind, its tattered fabric snapping against the eaves.

The villagers in the town below had warned him.

"Don't go up there, young master. That temple's been empty for twenty years. Bad luck."

"Strange things happen on that mountain. Strange things."

But Chen Yuan had no choice. The inn cost three silver coins a night. The temple cost nothing. And his family had sent him here to study, not to spend their last savings on a bed he couldn't afford.

He pushed open the gate. The hinges screamed.

The main hall was intact, at least. A dusty Buddha sat cross-legged on an altar, his serene face cracked down the middle like a broken mirror. Chen Yuan bowed to it anyway—his mother had raised him to be polite to gods and spirits alike—then began setting up his meager belongings. A brush. An inkstone. A stack of books tied with string. A single blanket rolled tight.

He lit a candle. The flame was small and weak against the growing dark.

Night came fast in the mountains.

By the time he'd cleared a space to sleep, the world outside was black as ink. Wind howled through gaps in the walls. Somewhere in the rafters, something scratched and skittered. Mice, he told himself. Just mice.

He tried to read. The characters blurred before his eyes.

Days of travel had exhausted him. He set the book aside, blew out the candle, and lay down on the hard wooden floor with his blanket wrapped around him like a cocoon. The last thing he saw before sleep took him was the cracked face of the Buddha, watching him in the dark.

---

He woke to knocking.

Three sharp raps on the temple door.

Chen Yuan sat up so fast his head spun. The candle had burned down to nothing. The moon hung outside the window, pale and cold, casting long shadows across the floor.

Knock. Knock. Knock.

Three more. Insistent. Rhythmic.

"Who's there?" His voice came out rough with sleep.

No answer.

He stood, his legs stiff from the hard floor, and walked to the door. His hand paused on the wooden bolt. His mother's voice echoed in his head: *Never open a door in the middle of the night, Yuan'er. Bad things wait on the other side of doors at night.*

But the knocking came again. Softer this time. Almost pleading.

"Please," a woman's voice said. "I'm lost. I saw your light."

A lost traveler. In the middle of the night. On a mountain path no one had used in years.

Chen Yuan's hand moved before his mind caught up. The bolt slid back. The door swung open.

She stood in the moonlight.

Her robes were white, plain and unadorned, but they seemed to glow in the silver light. Her hair fell loose around her shoulders, black as a raven's wing. Her face was beautiful in a way that made his chest ache—high cheekbones, full lips, eyes so dark they seemed to swallow the night.

"I'm sorry to disturb you," she said. Her voice was soft. Musical. "I was traveling to the next town, but I lost the path. The woods... they play tricks on me."

Chen Yuan opened his mouth to speak. Then he looked down.

Her shadow fell across the threshold.

It had nine tails.

They moved like living things, swaying and curling, long and graceful and *wrong*. A fox's tails. A spirit's tails.

He looked up at her face. She was watching him with those dark, endless eyes. Waiting.

"I..." His throat closed. "I don't..."

"You see them." It wasn't a question.

He nodded.

She sighed. It was a tired sound, old and worn. "Most humans don't. Not until it's too late." She tilted her head, studying him. "But you do. That's interesting."

"What are you?" The words came out before he could stop them.

"I'm cold," she said instead of answering. "I'm tired. And I've been running for three days without rest." She looked past him into the dark temple. "Will you let me stay? Just for tonight. I'll be gone before dawn."

His mind screamed at him to say no. This was a fox spirit. A creature of trickery and deception. His grandmother had told him stories about fox spirits—how they lured men to their deaths, how they fed on human life force, how they were beautiful and deadly and never to be trusted.

But her shadow had nine tails. And she'd told him the truth when she could have lied.

"I have nothing to offer you," he said slowly. "No food. No tea. Only a hard floor and a drafty room."

A smile flickered at the corner of her lips. It was small and quick, but it transformed her face. "That's more than I've had in weeks."

She stepped inside.

Chen Yuan closed the door behind her and bolted it, though he didn't know why. It wouldn't stop her if she meant to hurt him. Nothing would.

She sat down on the floor across from his blanket, folding her legs beneath her with a fluid grace that was not quite human. Her shadow stretched across the wall behind her, nine tails swaying gently. Watching her shadow was easier than watching her face.

"You're a scholar," she said.

"Yes."

"Studying for the exams?"

"Yes."

"From the city?"

"No. A village to the east. Small place. You wouldn't know it."

"Try me."

He told her. She nodded, a slight recognition in her eyes. "I passed through there once. Many years ago. There was a river with white stones."

"It's still there."

"Good." She looked around the temple with something like approval. "This is a quiet place. The spirits here are old and sleepy. They won't bother us."

"Us?"

She looked at him. Really looked at him. Her eyes traveled from his face to his hands to the books stacked beside him, and he felt like she was reading him faster than he could read himself.

"I'm hiding," she said. "There are men hunting me. Celestial exorcists. They've killed all the others."

"Others?"

"The Nine-Tailed. My family." Her voice was flat. Empty. "I'm the last one left."

Chen Yuan's breath stopped. The Nine-Tailed Fox lineage was legend—ancient spirits of unmatched power, tricksters and guardians and bringers of fortune or ruin depending on the tale. His grandmother had told him stories about them. She'd said they were as old as the mountains themselves.

Now this woman was telling him they were all dead.

"I'm sorry," he said.

She blinked. "You're sorry?"

"I don't know what else to say." He rubbed the back of his neck. "I've never met someone whose family was... hunted before."

"You've never met a fox spirit before."

"No. That too."

She laughed. It was a small sound, barely more than a breath, but it was genuine. "You're strange, Scholar."

"Chen Yuan. My name is Chen Yuan."

"Chen Yuan." She tested the name on her tongue. "I am Mei."

"Mei." He repeated it, and the syllable felt warm in his mouth. "Just Mei?"

"Just Mei." Her shadow tails curled behind her. "For now."

They sat in silence for a long moment. The wind howled outside. Somewhere in the dark, an owl called. The candle had long since burned out, but the moonlight streaming through the window was enough to see by. Enough to see her.

"Why are you telling me this?" he asked. "I'm a stranger. A human. I could run to the village and tell everyone what I saw."

"Could you?"

He thought about it. The villagers would believe him, maybe. Or they'd think he was mad. Either way, they'd come up the mountain with torches and pitchforks, and she would be gone before they arrived. And if the exorcists were hunting her, they'd be watching for exactly that kind of disturbance.

"No," he admitted. "I don't think I could."

"Because you're kind." She said it simply, like it was an obvious fact. "I knew it the moment I saw you. You opened your door to a stranger in the middle of the night. That's not stupidity. That's kindness."

"Or foolishness."

"Sometimes they're the same thing." She leaned back on her hands, her tails flickering in the shadows. "I have a proposal for you, Chen Yuan."

His heart stuttered. He'd heard stories about fox spirit bargains. They never ended well.

"I'm listening."

"I need a place to hide. You need someone to keep you company while you study." She smiled, and this time it was sharper. More fox-like. "One year. I'll stay here with you. I'll cook for you, clean for you, keep the bad spirits away. And at the end of that year, you can ask me for one wish. Anything you want."

"Anything?"

"Within reason. I'm powerful, but I'm not a god." She tilted her head. "You could ask for riches. Fame. Success in your exams. A beautiful wife." Her eyes glittered. "I could give you all of that."

"And what do you get?"

"Safety." The word was soft. Vulnerable. "The exorcists won't think to look for me here. A mortal scholar's mountain temple. They'll be searching the cities, the forests, the places where spirits gather. They won't look for me in a dusty old temple with a cracked Buddha and a kind young man."

Chen Yuan stared at her. Her shadow tails swayed. Her eyes were dark and old and tired.

One year. One wish.

The stories said never to bargain with fox spirits. They'd twist your words, trick you, trap you with loopholes and technicalities. But Mei hadn't hidden what she was. She'd told him the truth when she could have lied.

And she looked so tired.

"What if I refuse?"

"Then I'll leave. Find somewhere else to hide." She shrugged. "It's your choice, Scholar."

He thought about his empty stomach and his thin blanket and the long, lonely months ahead. He thought about the exam that would determine his future, the crushing weight of his family's expectations, the fear that he wasn't smart enough or good enough or strong enough.

He thought about her shadow tails, beautiful and haunting, and the way she'd said his name.

"I have conditions."

She raised an eyebrow.

"No tricks. No lies. If you want my help, you deal with me honestly."

"Fair."

"And when the year is up, you leave. No extensions, no arguments."

"Of course."

"And..." He hesitated. "You tell me your real name. Before you go."

Her eyes widened. Just a fraction. Just for a moment. Then she smiled, and it was the most human expression he'd seen on her face all night.

"Agreed."

She held out her hand. Her fingers were pale and slender, her nails perfectly shaped.

He took it.

Her skin was warm. Warmer than it should have been. Warm like sunlight.

"I accept your bargain, Chen Yuan."

"I accept yours, Mei."

And somewhere in the depths of the mountain, something ancient and sleeping stirred.

title: The Price of a Single Wish
content:
The morning light cut through the bamboo slats of Chen Yuan’s study like blades of gold. He sat at his desk, brush hovering over blank paper, the ink drying on its tip.

He hadn’t written a single character in three hours.

The fox woman—Mei—hadn’t moved from the window seat since dawn. She sat with her legs tucked beneath her, watching the mist curl through the mountain pines. Her hair spilled down her back like a river of ink, and the morning sun caught the edges of her profile in a way that made his chest ache.

“You’re staring,” she said without turning.

Heat crawled up his neck. “I was thinking.”

“Liar.” She finally looked at him, and her eyes held that strange amber glow, like honey catching fire. “You were wondering if I’m real.”

He set down his brush. “The thought crossed my mind.”

“And?”

“You’re still here.”

She smiled, but it didn’t reach her eyes. “I keep my bargains, Scholar Chen. One year of companionship. One wish granted. Those were the terms.”

He’d agreed last night, half-convinced he was dreaming. A fox spirit at his door, offering him anything he desired in exchange for twelve months of her company. Any scholar in the empire would have called him mad for hesitating.

But there was something in the way she’d said it—*one year*—that made him think the bargain favored her more than him.

“What happens after the year ends?” he asked.

She rose from the window seat, her movements fluid as water. The silk of her robes whispered against the floorboards. “You’ll get your wish. I’ll leave. You’ll forget me within a season.”

“Forget you?”

“Mortals do.” She drifted toward his bookshelf, trailing her fingers across the spines. “Fox spirits are like dreams. You remember the feeling long after the details fade. But eventually, even the feeling goes.”

She pulled a scroll from the shelf—a treatise on celestial bureaucracy by some long-dead magistrate. “You have good taste, Scholar. Most scholars I’ve known fill their shelves with examination primers and nothing else. No poetry. No history. No heart.”

“You’ve known many scholars?”

“Enough.” She unrolled the scroll, scanning the characters. “They’re all the same. Starving for knowledge. Starving for power. Starving for a woman’s touch. They make wishes, and I grant them, and they never understand what they’ve traded until it’s too late.”

A cold knot formed in his stomach. “What exactly do they trade?”

She looked up from the scroll, and for a moment, her expression was unreadable. Then she smiled again, and the warmth was back, but it felt like a mask. “That depends on the wish. A wish for wealth takes something else. A wish for love takes something else. A wish for power...” She let the words hang. “Those cost the most.”

“And what will my wish cost?”

“You haven’t made your wish yet, Scholar.” She set down the scroll and walked toward him, close enough that he could smell jasmine and rain. “That’s the beauty of my bargain. You have a full year to decide. And I have a full year to convince you that what you *think* you want isn’t what you *truly* need.”

She stopped a hand’s breadth from his desk. “So tell me, Chen Yuan. Why did you really come to this temple?”

The question caught him off guard. “I told you. To study for the examinations.”

“Lies.” She said it softly, without accusation. “A man who wants to pass the examinations studies in the capital, where he can network with officials and attend lectures by famous tutors. A man who wants to *hide* comes to a mountain temple with no neighbors for ten li and a path that washes out every spring.”

He opened his mouth. Closed it.

She was right.

He’d told himself the isolation would help him focus. That the quiet would let him memorize the classics without distraction. But the truth was simpler and uglier.

He’d run.

“My father was a magistrate,” he said slowly. “A good one. Fair. He believed in the law and in justice. When a landowner’s son killed a peasant girl and claimed it was an accident, my father investigated. He found witnesses. He found proof. He did his duty.”

Mei’s expression went still.

“The landowner had connections in the capital,” he continued. “My father was accused of accepting bribes. They stripped him of his rank. Seized our property. He died in prison before the trial even began.”

“And you?”

“I was supposed to continue his legacy.” His voice cracked on the word. “Pass the examinations. Become an official. Clear his name. That was his dying wish.”

“But you’re here. Hiding.”

“Because I found something in his papers.” He pulled a letter from beneath his inkstone—creased, yellowed, stained with tea and tears. “This arrived three days after his death. I don’t know who sent it. There was no seal, no signature.”

He held it out to her. She took it, her fingers brushing his, and the touch sent a shiver up his arm.

She read in silence. When she finished, her face had gone pale.

The letter said: *Your father died because he asked too many questions. So will you, if you continue his path. The mountain temple. One year. Stay silent, or join him.*

“Who wrote this?” Mei asked.

“I don’t know.”

“And you came here because of a threat?”

“I came here because I’m a coward.” The words tasted like ash. “I came here because I’m not my father. He faced the truth and died for it. I ran.”

Mei folded the letter carefully, precisely, and handed it back. Her amber eyes met his, and for the first time since she’d arrived, he saw something genuine in them—not warmth, not playfulness, but recognition.

“You think you’re the only one hiding?” she asked.

“You’re a fox spirit. What do you have to hide from?”

She laughed, but it was bitter. “The Celestial Exorcists. The Heavenly Bureau. Every demon hunter in the Nine Provinces who wants the last Nine-Tailed Fox’s core for their collection.”

“Last?”

“My clan is dead.” She said it flatly, like she was reading a tax record. “Hunted. Skinned. Their cores harvested and sold to cultivators who wanted to cheat their way to immortality. I’m the only one left.”

The knot in his stomach tightened. “Is that why you came here? To hide with a mortal?”

“To hide with *you*.” She emphasized the word. “Not just any mortal. A scholar with a death threat. A man who’s already being watched by powerful enemies. The Celestial Exorcists don’t notice fox spirits hiding in the shadow of a bigger hunt.”

He stared at her. “You’re using me.”

“Of course I’m using you.” She said it like it was obvious. “And you’re using me. You get a year of companionship, a wish, and maybe—if you’re clever—someone who can help you survive whatever’s coming for you. I get a hiding place. We both get what we want.”

“And when the year ends?”

She looked away. “When the year ends, we both get what we deserve.”

The silence stretched between them, heavy and sharp.

Then she turned back to him, and her mask was firmly in place—the playful fox spirit, the seductress, the trickster. “But that’s a year away, Scholar. Today, I’m hungry, and you have rice, and I’m told I make an excellent congee.”

She was already walking toward the kitchen before he could respond.

He sat there, alone in his study, the threatening letter cold in his hands. The morning had started with a beautiful woman in his window seat and a bargain that seemed too good to be true.

Now he knew why.

She was hiding from celestial hunters. He was hiding from mortal ones. And somewhere in the shadows between them, someone—or something—was waiting.

He folded the letter and tucked it into his sleeve.

The congee was good. Better than good—it was the best thing he’d eaten in months, rich and savory, with wild mushrooms she’d gathered from the forest floor while he’d been lost in his papers. She ate across from him, her chopsticks moving with an elegance that seemed unconscious, natural.

“You’re doing it again,” she said.

“Staring?”

“Thinking too loud.” She dipped her head toward the window. “There’s a storm coming. Feel the air.”

He hadn’t noticed. But now that she mentioned it, the air was heavy, charged, like the moment before thunder breaks.

“How do you know?”

“Fox senses.” She smiled. “Also, the birds all left an hour ago. Mortal sign. You should learn to read the world around you, Scholar. It tells you more than any book.”

He wanted to ask more—about her clan, about the Celestial Exorcists, about the exact terms of their bargain. But something in her expression warned him not to push.

Instead, he asked: “Will you teach me?”

She paused, chopsticks halfway to her mouth. “Teach you what?”

“To read the world. The signs. The things I don’t see.”

Her eyes narrowed. “That’s not part of the bargain.”

“I know.” He met her gaze. “Consider it an additional request. Not a wish—just a request. From someone who’s spent too long with his head in books and not enough time looking at what’s right in front of him.”

She studied him for a long moment. Then she set down her chopsticks.

“One condition.”

“Name it.”

“When I teach you to see,” she said, “you won’t look away from what you find. And you won’t lie to me about what you see.”

He nodded. “Agreed.”

She extended her hand across the table. “Then we have a new bargain.”

He took it. Her skin was warm, soft, but beneath it he felt something else—a pulse of energy, like heat from a banked fire. The touch lingered a heartbeat longer than necessary.

Then she pulled away.

“Finish your congee,” she said. “We start tonight.”

“Tonight? But the storm—”

“Exactly.” Her smile was sharp. “Storms are when secrets come out to play.”

She rose and glided toward the door, her robes trailing behind her like smoke. At the threshold, she paused.

“And Chen Yuan?”

“Yes?”

“The person who sent that letter.” Her voice dropped, losing all playfulness. “They’re closer than you think. I can smell them on the path.”

The door clicked shut behind her.

Chen Yuan sat alone in the sudden silence, the congee cooling in his bowl, the storm wind rattling the window frames.

The first thunder rolled across the mountains.

And somewhere in the distance, a twig snapped.

Someone was coming up the path.

title: The Taste of Memory
content:
Chen Yuan woke to the smell of smoke.

Not the clean smoke of cooking fires or the sharp smoke of autumn leaves burning. This was thick and cloying, carrying the weight of incense and something else. Something that made his skin prickle before his eyes opened.

He sat up in bed. Moonlight painted silver patterns across his floor. The brazier sat cold in the corner, untouched since he'd banked it at midnight. And yet the room swam with gray haze, curling around his desk, his books, his ink stone.

Mei stood at his window.

She hadn't done that before. In three nights since she'd appeared, she had always waited for him to speak first, always arranged herself at a proper distance, always watched him with those amber eyes that seemed to know things they shouldn't.

Tonight, her back was to him. Her white dress hung loose at her shoulders. Her hair, unbound, fell past her waist like a waterfall of ink. And through her body, he could see the faint outline of the moon.

"Mei?"

She turned.

The woman who looked at him was not the woman who had bargained with him.

Her eyes still held that fox-gold, but now twin flames danced in their depths. Her skin glowed with an internal light, pale as porcelain held before a candle. And her smile — her smile stretched just a fraction too wide, showing teeth that seemed sharper than they'd been three days ago.

"Scholar Chen." Her voice came from everywhere. "I have a confession."

He reached for his robes. His fingers found the cloth, but his hands wouldn't move properly. They trembled like leaves in a storm.

"What kind of confession?"

"The kind that might send you running." She stepped toward him. Her feet didn't make sound against the floorboards. "The kind that makes me a danger to everyone I meet."

"You said you were hiding from celestial exorcists."

"I said I was hunted." She stopped at the foot of his bed. The smoke swirled around her ankles like living things. "I didn't say why."

Chen Yuan had read enough strange tales to know what came next. The fox spirit who had fed on human hearts. The fox spirit who had stolen a child's life force. The fox spirit who had driven a village mad with grief and desire.

He should be afraid.

He was afraid.

But beneath the fear, something else stirred. Something that had been sleeping since he'd buried his father two winters ago. Something that looked at this creature of smoke and moonlight and thought: *At least she is honest about what she is.*

"Tell me."

Mei's smile faded. For a moment, she looked almost human. Almost fragile.

"I am the last of the Nine-Tailed Fox lineage." She said the words like they were stones she'd been carrying. "My mother was the Ninth Queen of the Fox Court. My father was a storm god who forgot his name. I was born with nine tails and a throne that would never be mine."

"You have one tail."

"I have one tail *now*." Her hand moved to her spine, pressing against the space between her shoulder blades. "The celestial court didn't just hunt my family. They devoured us. My mother's power. My sisters' grace. My aunts' wisdom. They took it all and sealed it in a jade prison that floats somewhere between heaven and earth."

"Then how are you here?"

"Because I ran." Her voice cracked. "Because when the exorcists came for me, I cut off eight of my tails and scattered them across the mortal realm. Each tail holds a piece of my power. Each tail is a trail of breadcrumbs leading to my death."

"But you're here. In my temple."

"You're a scholar." She met his eyes. "The celestial court doesn't care about scholars. They care about cultivators, about immortals who might threaten their throne. You're beneath their notice."

"Thank you?"

"It's not a compliment." Her voice hardened. "It's a strategy. I chose you because you were nothing. Because your Qi is as weak as a candle flame. Because the celestial exorcists would never think to look for me in the shadow of a man who will die in sixty years without ever achieving greatness."

The words hit like stones.

Chen Yuan had known he was ordinary. He'd known it since he was a child, watching his father struggle to afford ink, watching his mother sell her hairpins for rice. He'd known it when he'd failed the provincial exam three times, when his teachers had sighed and told him to try harder, when the village elders had stopped expecting anything from him.

Knowing it and hearing it were different things.

"Is that why you chose me?" His voice came out steadier than he expected. "Because I'm worthless?"

Mei flinched.

The smoke around her feet dissipated. The glow in her eyes dimmed. She looked at him, and for the first time, she seemed to actually *see* him.

"No." Her voice dropped to a whisper. "I chose you because you were kind."

"The kitten."

"I was watching you for three days before I showed myself." She sat on the edge of his bed. The ropes groaned under her weight. "I saw you give your last coin to that beggar woman. I saw you share your rice with a stranger. I saw you bury that kitten with your own hands and weep for a creature that had never done anything for you."

"Those were small things."

"They were everything." Her hand reached for his. Her fingers were cold. "In the fox court, kindness was weakness. Compassion was a wound. My mother taught me to see humans as tools, as vessels for power, as entertainment. But you — you loved a kitten that couldn't love you back."

Chen Yuan looked at their hands. Her fingers interlaced with his. His skin crawled where she touched him, but the sensation wasn't fear. It was something else. Something that made his heart beat faster and his thoughts scatter like startled birds.

"What did you wish for?" she asked. "When you made the bargain."

"I told you. I wished for companionship."

"No." Her grip tightened. "I've lived with you for three days. You don't talk about your family. You don't talk about your past. You study until your eyes bleed and you pray to a god you don't believe in. That's not a man who wants companionship. That's a man who wants to escape."

The incense smoke thickened.

Chen Yuan tried to pull his hand away. She held fast.

"Let me go."

"Not until you tell me the truth."

"What does it matter?" His voice rose. "You're leaving in a year. You'll find your tails and your power and your throne, and I'll be dead in sixty years without achieving greatness. Why does my wish matter?"

"Because I've seen your soul, Scholar Chen." Her eyes blazed. "And it's not the soul of an ordinary man."

The words hung between them like a drawn blade.

Chen Yuan felt the world tilt. The walls of the temple seemed to press closer. The smoke coiled around his throat, and for a moment — just a moment — he saw something in Mei's eyes that made his blood run cold.

Not malice. Not hunger.

Recognition.

She knew him. She knew something about him that he didn't know himself.

"What do you mean?" His voice came out thin.

Mei opened her mouth.

The temple door exploded inward.

Wood splintered. Dust flew. A beam of golden light cut through the smoke like a sword through silk, and in its center stood a man in white robes, holding a talisman that burned with celestial fire.

"Fox spirit." His voice rolled like thunder. "Release the mortal, and I will grant you a quick death."

Mei was on her feet before Chen Yuan could blink. Her hand still held his, and she pulled him behind her with a strength that shouldn't have fit her small frame.

"Chen Yuan." Her voice was no longer soft, no longer hesitant. It was the voice of a creature who had survived when all her kin had died. "When I let go, you will run. You will run to the back of the temple, and you will not stop until you reach the river."

"The river is three miles away."

"Then you'd better run fast."

The exorcist raised his talisman. The golden light grew blinding. The walls of the temple began to smoke where the light touched them.

"I said release him."

"He chose me." Mei's voice rang with power. "He made a bargain. He is mine to protect."

"He is mortal. You are a creature of the wild. The celestial court has decreed—"

"The celestial court can burn."

She let go of Chen Yuan's hand.

The air split apart.

Where Mei stood, a creature of light and fury rose. Her white dress became fur. Her hands became claws. Behind her, where there should have been nine tails, there was only one — but that one tail blazed like a comet, trailing starlight and smoke.

But the exorcist's talisman blazed brighter.

"Last of the Nine-Tailed Fox lineage." The exorcist smiled. "Do you know how long I've searched for you?"

"Long enough to forget your manners."

"Long enough to know your weakness." The exorcist's eyes shifted to Chen Yuan. "She's attached to you, mortal. How touching. How unfortunate."

The golden light shifted.

Chen Yuan didn't see the attack coming. He only felt Mei's body slam into his, felt her arms wrap around him, felt her warmth and her cold and her impossible strength as she threw them both through the temple wall.

They tumbled into the night air. Stars spun. Mountains rushed up to meet them. Mei's claws dug into his robes as she twisted them in midair, and they hit the ground — not hard stone, but soft forest earth, damp with dew and shadow.

Mei gasped.

Chen Yuan pushed himself up. His hands were shaking. His heart was screaming.

The temple sat above them, burning with golden light.

"I can't run." Mei's voice was barely a whisper. "He'll find me. He'll always find me."

"Then what do we do?"

She looked at him. In the darkness, her eyes were two dying embers.

"We make a new bargain, Scholar Chen." Her hand found his. "Hold on to me. Don't let go. No matter what you see."

"What will I see?"

"Everything." Her fingers interlaced with his. "You'll see everything I've ever been. Everything I've ever done. Every death I've caused. Every life I've failed to save."

"Why would you show me that?"

"Because if you're going to die for me," she said, "you deserve to know what you're dying for."

The golden light crested the hill.

Mei pulled him into the darkness.

And Chen Yuan saw.

title: The Blood on His Hands
content:
The rain came three days later.

Chen Yuan sat at his desk, brush suspended over blank paper. The characters wouldn't come. They scattered like startled birds every time he tried to form them. Outside, water drummed against the temple tiles in a rhythm that matched his heartbeat.

Too fast. Too loud.

Mei had been gone since dawn.

She left every morning now, slipping out before the first light touched the mountain peaks. She never said where she went. He never asked. Some questions felt like knives—once you picked them up, you couldn't put them down without drawing blood.

The brush snapped in his grip.

He stared at the broken pieces in his palm. The ink had stained his fingers black, seeping into the lines of his palm like old rivers carved into a map. He'd had that brush since he was twelve. His mother had given it to him, along with her last words.

*Study hard, Yuan. Become someone worthy of this family's name.*

He hadn't. He was a failed scholar living in a crumbling temple, sharing his bed with a fox spirit while celestial hunters combed the mountains for her.

His mother would have wept.

The door slid open.

Mei stood in the frame, her robes soaked through, her dark hair plastered to her face. Rain dripped from her chin. She looked like a drowned ghost, beautiful and terrible in the gray light.

"You're still awake," she said.

"I haven't slept."

She stepped inside, leaving puddles on the floorboards. The fire in the brazier hissed as she passed, as if even the flames recoiled from her cold.

"Good." She knelt across from him, her movements slow, deliberate. "We need to talk."

Chen Yuan set down the broken brush. "About what?"

"The hunters. They're closer now. I can feel them."

A cold knot tightened in his chest. "How close?"

"Three days. Maybe two." She met his eyes, and for the first time since she'd appeared at his door, he saw something other than mischief or hunger in her gaze. Fear. "They have a tracker. A celestial hound. It's following my scent."

"Then leave."

The words came out before he could stop them. He didn't know why he'd said them. He didn't want her to leave. He wanted to grab her wrists, press her hands to his chest, beg her to stay until the hunters came and burned the temple down around them.

But he'd said it. And now it hung in the air between them, sharp and final.

Mei tilted her head, studying him like she was reading a book written in a language only she understood. "You want me to go?"

"No."

"Then why—"

"Because I can't protect you."

She laughed. It wasn't a kind sound. "Protect me? Scholar, I've lived three hundred years. I've outrun exorcists, outwitted heavenly officials, and bedded a dragon prince who still sends me letters begging for another night. You think I need your protection?"

"Then why are you here?"

The question cracked open the space between them. For a long moment, she said nothing. The rain filled the silence, drowning the world in gray.

"You know what they'll do if they catch me?" she finally asked.

"I've read the stories."

"No." Her voice dropped, soft and raw. "You've read children's tales. You've read scratched warnings meant to frighten farmers and scholars. You don't know what they'll do."

She pulled back her sleeve.

Chen Yuan's breath caught.

Her arm was covered in scars. Not the pale lines of old wounds, but fresh burns, black and cracked, weeping a faint golden fluid that steamed in the cold air. The marks spiraled up her wrist, disappearing beneath her robe.

"What—"

"Celestial chains." She let the sleeve fall. "They burn when the hunter gets close. They tighten. Eventually, they'll sink into my bones and hold me still while they carve out my cores."

"Cores?"

"My cultivation. My power. My lives." She smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "A nine-tailed fox has nine chances at immortality. They take them one by one. Each one pulled out is more painful than the last. By the seventh, you're begging for death. By the eighth, you forget you ever had a name."

The fire crackled. The rain kept falling.

Chen Yuan stared at the spot where her scars had been, at the fabric that now hid them again. He wanted to reach out, touch her wrist, feel her pulse beneath his fingers. He wanted to promise her safety, even though he knew promises meant nothing to creatures like her.

"What does your wish have to do with this?" she asked.

"What?"

"Your wish. The bargain. You said you had one. You haven't told me what it is."

He looked away.

"Chen Yuan." She leaned forward, her voice dropping to a whisper. "What did you wish for?"

The words wouldn't come. They were stuck somewhere deep in his chest, tangled around his ribs like roots around buried bones. He couldn't say them. If he said them, they would become real. And if they became real, she would leave.

Or worse.

She would stay.

"Not yet," he said. "The year isn't up."

"You're afraid."

"Of course I'm afraid."

"No." She reached across the table and pressed her palm against his chest. Her hand was cold, but the warmth of her touch seeped through his robes, sinking into his skin like sunlight through clouds. "Not of the hunters. Not of dying. You're afraid of what you're becoming."

He grabbed her wrist.

His grip was too tight. He knew it was too tight. But he couldn't let go. Her pulse beat against his fingers, fast and steady, a rhythm that belonged to something older than the mountains around them.

"I've always been this," he said. "I just didn't know it until you came."

Her eyes widened.

The fire guttered. The rain stopped.

And in the silence, something shifted inside Chen Yuan's chest. Something ancient. Something hungry. Something that had been sleeping beneath his ribs since the day he was born, waiting for the right moment to wake.

Mei pulled her hand back.

"What are you?" she whispered.

He opened his mouth to answer.

The temple door exploded inward.

Wood splintered. Wind howled through the gap, extinguishing the fire and plunging the room into darkness. Chen Yuan scrambled to his feet, his heart slamming against his ribs. Mei grabbed his arm, her grip iron-tight.

"Stay behind me," she said.

"No—"

"Scholar. For once in your life. Do what you're told."

A figure stepped through the shattered doorway. Tall. Wrapped in shadows that moved like living things. Two eyes burned in the darkness, gold and cold, fixed on Mei with the patience of a hunter who had been tracking his prey for a very long time.

"Mei of the Nine Tails." The voice was smooth, layered, like silk wrapped around steel. "You've led me on a fine chase."

The hunter stepped into what little light remained.

He was beautiful. That was the first thing Chen Yuan noticed. Beautiful in the way a blade is beautiful—sharp, perfect, meant to cut. His robes were white, unmarked by rain or mud. His hair fell in a silver cascade past his shoulders. And in his right hand, he held a sword made of light, its edge humming with a sound that made Chen Yuan's teeth ache.

"Who are you?" Chen Yuan demanded.

The hunter's golden eyes slid to him. For a moment, something flickered in their depths. Recognition. Surprise. And then—amusement.

"You don't know."

"Know what?"

The hunter laughed. It was a beautiful sound, like wind chimes in a storm.

"Scholar Chen Yuan," he said. "Son of Chen Wei, the Demon Vanquisher. Grandson of Chen Tian, the Star-Swallower. Heir to the bloodline that has hunted celestial spirits for a thousand years."

The world tilted.

Chen Yuan's legs gave out. He hit the floor hard, his knees cracking against the wood. Mei's hand was still on his arm, but he couldn't feel it. He couldn't feel anything except the words echoing in his skull, over and over, like a bell that refused to stop ringing.

*Heir to the bloodline that has hunted celestial spirits for a thousand years.*

"You're lying," he said.

The hunter's smile widened. "Am I?"

"Chen Yuan." Mei's voice came from far away. "Chen Yuan, look at me."

He looked.

Her face was pale. Her eyes were wide, searching his like she was trying to find the scholar she'd shared tea with, the man who stuttered when she smiled, the mortal who had offered her shelter without asking for anything in return.

"Did you know?" she asked.

"No. I swear—"

"Did you know?"

"No!"

The hunter stepped forward. The sword of light hummed louder, casting strange shadows across the walls. "He doesn't. How tragic. The last of the Chen bloodline, unaware of his own nature. Your family would be ashamed."

"Shut up," Chen Yuan snarled.

The hunter raised an eyebrow. "The cub has teeth."

"I said shut up."

He got to his feet. His legs were shaking. His hands were shaking. Everything inside him was shaking, except for that thing that had woken up when Mei touched him. That thing was still. That thing was waiting.

"Mei," he said. "Run."

"Chen Yuan—"

"Run. Now."

She grabbed his face, forcing him to meet her eyes. Her hands were warm against his cheeks. Her breath was sweet, like jasmine blooming in moonlight.

"I'm not leaving you," she said.

"You have to."

"I'm not leaving you."

The hunter sighed. "How touching. But I'm afraid I have a quota to fill."

He raised his sword.

Chen Yuan moved.

He didn't think. He didn't plan. His body simply acted, stepping in front of Mei, raising his arm to block the blow that should have cut him in half.

The sword stopped.

An inch from his throat.

The hunter's golden eyes widened.

"No," he breathed. "It can't be."

Chen Yuan looked down.

His arm was covered in light. Not the hunter's light. Something else. Something that came from inside him, pouring out of his skin in rivers of gold and silver, curling around his body like a second skin.

The scars on his palm—the ones he'd had since birth, the ones his mother said were birthmarks—were glowing.

"You," the hunter whispered, "are the Star-Swallower reborn."

Chen Yuan didn't have time to process the words.

Because behind him, Mei screamed.

title: The Wound That Would Not Close
content:
The blood dripped through Chen Yuan's fingers and splattered against the wooden floor in small, dark blossoms.

Mei stood in the doorway of his study, her silver hair loose around her shoulders, her eyes fixed on the crimson trail that led from the entrance to where he knelt. The fox fur she usually wore as a stole was gone. In its place, a simple white robe that made her look almost human.

Almost.

"You're hurt," she said.

It wasn't a question.

Chen Yuan pressed his palm harder against his ribs. The bandages he'd wrapped around himself an hour ago were already soaked through. The wound had stopped bleeding three times now. Three times, it had started again, as if some memory in his flesh refused to let the injury heal.

"A disagreement in the village," he said. "Some men from the north. They didn't appreciate a scholar telling them their philosophy was flawed."

Mei stepped closer. Her bare feet made no sound. "You argued philosophy with northern mercenaries?"

"I argued that burning a farmer's house because he couldn't pay protection money was philosophically unsound. They disagreed. Vehemently."

She knelt beside him, her movements fluid as water. Her hand reached for his, and he let her pull it away from the wound. The bandages were dark red now, the fabric clinging to his skin.

"This needs proper cleaning," she said. "I'll fetch water."

"Don't."

The word came out sharper than he intended. She paused, her fingers still touching his wrist.

"The wound," he said, and stopped. How to explain? How to tell her that every time he'd tried to clean it, the edges of the cut had smoked and sizzled as if the water itself was poison? How to explain that the blood on his hands smelled wrong—copper and something else, something that reminded him of incense burned in temples he'd never visited?

"I'll help you," Mei said softly. "But you must trust me."

Chen Yuan laughed. It came out broken. "Trust a fox spirit? The stories say you eat men's souls."

"The stories say a lot of things." She rose and walked to the small table where his brush and ink sat. She dipped his finest brush into the inkstone, then returned to him. "The stories say scholars are virtuous and poor. You have three robes, all silk, and your calligraphy set costs more than a farmer's year. The stories lie."

She drew a character on his forehead. The ink burned cold, and the throbbing in his side eased slightly.

"What was that?"

"A warding mark. To keep the wound from worsening." She sat back on her heels. "Now. Let me see what's inside."

Chen Yuan hesitated. But the blood kept coming, and the cold burn of her mark had given him a sliver of relief. He nodded.

She unwound the bandages slowly. Each layer peeled away with the sound of wet paper tearing. When the last strip fell free, she went still.

The wound was a gash across his ribs, three fingers wide and deep enough to show glimpses of white bone beneath. But that wasn't what made her breath catch.

The edges of the cut shimmered. Faint golden light pulsed from the torn flesh, growing brighter with each heartbeat.

"That's not possible," she whispered.

"Is that your professional opinion as a spirit of the wild?"

She didn't laugh at his joke. She was staring at the wound with an expression he'd never seen on her face before. Fear. Not the playful fear she sometimes feigned when he caught her stealing his wine. Real fear.

"Mei. What is it?"

"This isn't an ordinary wound." Her fingers hovered over the cut, not quite touching. "The golden light. That's celestial essence. Only heavenly beings have it. Only—" She stopped. Her eyes snapped to his. "Your blood smells like temple incense. Your shadow doesn't move the way it should in candlelight. I thought it was just odd mortal things, but—"

"But what?"

"Chen Yuan. What are you?"

The question hung between them. He should have an answer. He was Chen Yuan, twenty-three years old, son of a minor scholar-official from the eastern provinces. He'd passed the county exams at sixteen, the prefectural at nineteen. He'd come to this mountain temple to study for the imperial examinations. He was mortal. He was ordinary. He was—

He was lying to himself.

"I don't know," he said, and the truth of it made his chest ache.

Something moved in the shadows beyond the temple door. Mei's head snapped up. Her ears—when had her ears become pointed?—twitched.

"Someone's coming."

"More mercenaries?"

"No. Worse." She grabbed his arm and pulled him to his feet. The movement sent fresh pain through his side, and he bit back a cry. "They're here. The celestial exorcists. They followed my trail."

"The exorcists? But I thought you said the warding marks would—"

"I said they would hide me from ordinary hunters." She was already moving, gathering her few possessions, stuffing them into a cloth bundle. "These are not ordinary. They're from the Heavenly Court. They've been hunting my kind for centuries."

"Then run. I'll delay them."

She stopped. Turned. Looked at him with those amber eyes that seemed to see straight through his skin.

"You're bleeding golden light. You have celestial essence in your veins. And you want to face the Heavenly Court's exorcists alone?"

"I'm a scholar. What can they do to a scholar?"

"They can kill you." She crossed to him and pressed her palm flat against his chest. "They can kill you and not even know what they've destroyed."

Her hand was warm. He could feel the pulse of her spirit through his skin, a rhythm that matched his own heartbeat. When had that happened? When had their hearts begun to beat together?

"The bargain," he said. "One year of companionship for one wish. I haven't made my wish yet."

"And you think now is the time to discuss this?"

"If I die tonight, I want you to know." He covered her hand with his. "I wasn't going to wish for wealth. Or power. Or success in the exams."

"Then what?"

The temple door splintered. Wood fragments exploded inward as a blade of pure white light sliced through the wood. A figure stepped through the wreckage—a man in white robes, his face hidden behind a porcelain mask painted with the face of a smiling bodhisattva.

"Fox spirit," the figure said. His voice was calm. Pleasant. The voice of a man discussing tea. "You've led us on a merry chase. But all chases end."

Mei pushed Chen Yuan behind her. Her form flickered—human, then fox, then something between the two. Nine tails of silver light erupted from her spine, filling the room with their glow.

"Run," she said, not looking at him. "Run and don't look back."

"Mei—"

"I said run!"

The exorcist raised his hand. White light gathered in his palm, condensing into a spear of pure divine energy. The air screamed as it flew.

Chen Yuan moved before he knew what he was doing. He stepped in front of Mei. The spear struck him in the chest.

White hot. Cold. Nothing. Everything.

He looked down at the spear of light embedded in his heart. It should have killed him. It should have turned him to ash.

Instead, the spear dissolved. The light flowed into him, into his blood, into his bones. And he remembered.

He remembered falling. Remembered the taste of stars. Remembered a crime so ancient that even the heavens had forgotten it. Remembered being cast out, stripped of wings, stripped of name, made mortal as punishment.

Chen Yuan.

Not a scholar.

A fallen god.

The golden light exploded from his body, and the temple roof tore away.
